chapters.title: Chapter 1: Introduction to the Energy Transition Landscape

lessons.title: Lesson 1: The Energy Transition In Context
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson explores how a just transition is about more than just technical solutions or policy frameworks; it is about addressing deep-rooted socio-economic disparities, fostering participatory governance, and empowering all stakeholders to engage in the transition process.
lessons.description: This lesson explores how a just transition is about more than just technical solutions or policy frameworks; it is about addressing deep-rooted socio-economic disparities, fostering participatory governance, and empowering all stakeholders to engage in the transition process.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Unpacking The Concept Of A Just Energy Transition
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson outlines the just energy transition, focusing on fairness, equity, and inclusivity in sustainable energy shifts. Originating from labor rights, it addresses historical inequities through environmental, climate, and energy justice. It highlights localized solutions and global frameworks like the AJET principles to balance urgent climate action with social and economic justice.

lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson delves into the specific challenges and opportunities that developing countries face in the context of the just energy transition. By examining these dynamics, we aim to shed light on strategies and initiatives that can facilitate a transition towards a more sustainable and equitable energy future for all.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Social Safety Nets and Income Support

It highlights targeted subsidies to reduce inequalities, gender-sensitive policies for inclusive access, and the role of these programs in poverty reduction and social stability during shifts to sustainable energy.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: A JET that Preserves Nature and Catalyzes Economic Growth

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Critical Minerals and Materials
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson addresses critical minerals essential for renewable energy technologies, highlighting challenges like rising demand, geographic concentration, and environmental/social impacts. It emphasizes the need for responsible extraction practices to bridge gaps between policies and on-ground outcomes. While offering economic opportunities for resource-rich nations, it underscores risks of resource mismanagement and calls for international cooperation to ensure equitable governance, sustainable development, and a just energy transition.
lessons.description: This lesson addresses critical minerals essential for renewable energy technologies, highlighting challenges like rising demand, geographic concentration, and environmental/social impacts. It emphasizes the need for responsible extraction practices to bridge gaps between policies and on-ground outcomes. While offering economic opportunities for resource-rich nations, it underscores risks of resource mismanagement and calls for international cooperation to ensure equitable governance, sustainable development, and a just energy transition.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Environmental Concerns of Clean Energy Technology Deployment
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson examines environmental concerns of renewable energy technologies like wind, solar, and hydropower, highlighting challenges such as land use, habitat disruption, water consumption, and hazardous materials. While lifecycle assessments show renewables have significantly lower GHG emissions than fossil fuels, the lesson stresses the need for mitigation strategies (e.g., turbine placement, solar panel recycling, habitat protection) to minimize ecological impacts and ensure sustainable deployment.
lessons.description: This lesson examines environmental concerns of renewable energy technologies like wind, solar, and hydropower, highlighting challenges such as land use, habitat disruption, water consumption, and hazardous materials. lessons.title: Lesson 3: Advancing the Value Chain
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson examines advancing the value chain in critical minerals and battery production, emphasizing opportunities for developing nations to shift from raw material exports to high-value processing. It highlights regional collaboration, vertical integration, and sustainable practices (e.g., recycling) to address socio-environmental impacts. Strategic governance and public-private partnerships are underscored as key drivers for economic resilience and equitable growth in the global energy transition.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Community-Led Sustainable Energy Projects and Community-Based Solutions
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson underscores the importance of proactive planning and strategic foresight in navigating the energy transition amid global crises like cost-of-living pressures and geopolitical instability. It highlights the need for collaborative governance and equitable infrastructure transformation to address historical inequities and build resilient systems

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Just Energy Transition Partnerships (JET-P)
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson explores Just Energy Transition Partnerships (JET-Ps) as innovative financing mechanisms to support developing nations in shifting from fossil fuels to clean energy. It emphasizes blending public and private capital to phase out coal, upgrade grids, and retrain workers. Challenges include ensuring equity, avoiding debt traps, and integrating grassroots priorities into national strategies. Effective governance and inclusive frameworks are stressed to balance economic shifts with social justice and accountability.

Effective governance and inclusive frameworks are stressed to balance economic shifts with social justice and accountability.

chapters.title: Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Energy Governance

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Introduction to Energy Governance
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson introduces energy governance as a critical foundation for achieving just and sustainable energy transitions. It highlights how effective, inclusive, and accountable governance systems are essential to overcome challenges like fossil fuel lock-in, market failures, and energy inequality, ensuring that the benefits of the transition are equitably shared and aligned with long-term climate goals.
lessons.description: This lesson introduces energy governance as a critical foundation for achieving just and sustainable energy transitions. It highlights how effective, inclusive, and accountable governance systems are essential to overcome challenges like fossil fuel lock-in, market failures, and energy inequality, ensuring that the benefits of the transition are equitably shared and aligned with long-term climate goals.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Actors in Sustainable Energy Governance
lessons.aiPrompt: The transition to sustainable energy systems represents one of the most pressing and complex challenges of the 21st century. Addressing this multifaceted imperative effectively demands a paradigm shift from traditional, siloed governance models to a comprehensive Whole-of-Society Approach (WSA)his lesson provides an examination of the WSA in sustainable energy governance, defining its core principles, identifying key stakeholders, and elucidating the profound benefits it offers.

Discussing the actors in energy governance, the chapter emphasizes that energy transitions succeed not just through technology or investment, but through strong, participatory governance systems that engage all levels of society and ensure fair, transparent, and adaptive decision-making.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Pillars of Good Energy Governance

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Designing Effective Policy And Regulatory Frameworks
lessons.aiPrompt:
Effective energy policies are the backbone of a successful and just energy transition. They guide investment, shape behavior, ensure access, and balance the interests of people, government, and industry. But policies often fail, not because of bad intentions, but because they ignore political realities, lack clear goals, or are poorly designed. 
This module explores how to design effective and context-sensitive energy policies and regulations by focusing on tools like Institutional and Context Analysis (ICA), Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA), and adaptive policymaking. It emphasizes the importance of aligning policies across sectors, anticipating future challenges through strategic foresight, and continuously refining regulations based on evidence, feedback, and evolving conditions.

It emphasizes the importance of aligning policies across sectors, anticipating future challenges through strategic foresight, and continuously refining regulations based on evidence, feedback, and evolving conditions.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Ensuring Transparency and Conducting Regular Oversight
lessons.aiPrompt: Transparency and oversight are essential for building trust, improving accountability, and ensuring that energy policies are fair, efficient, and effective. This lesson explores how open data, accessible information, and strong monitoring systems help prevent corruption, improve decision-making, and attract investment. It introduces key tools like internal and external audits, gender audits, and strategic foresight to strengthen public trust and ensure that no one is left behind in the energy transition

lessons.title: Lesson 3: A Holistic Energy Governance Framework
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson introduces a practical governance framework that connects core governance principles (like participation, transparency, and oversight) to key elements of the energy transition, such as infrastructure, finance, and social impacts. Using Nigeria’s experience as an example, we explore how this framework helps identify gaps, align institutions, and ensure no one is left behind.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Key Sustainable Energy Policy Instruments And Tool

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Planning and Strategic Instruments
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson introduces key national instruments used to plan and implement sustainable energy transitions, including long-term strategies, national energy policies, and sectoral action plans. It examines how countries set energy and climate goals, align them with development priorities, and operationalize them through tools like renewable energy plans, efficiency strategies, and clean cooking roadmaps.
lessons.description: This lesson introduces key national instruments used to plan and implement sustainable energy transitions, including long-term strategies, national energy policies, and sectoral action plans. It examines how countries set energy and climate goals, align them with development priorities, and operationalize them through tools like renewable energy plans, efficiency strategies, and clean cooking roadmaps.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Command-And- Control Instruments
lessons.aiPrompt: Regulations form the backbone of the energy transition by setting clear rules that shape how energy is produced, distributed, and consumed. This lesson explores command-and-control instruments which set enforceable targets to drive clean energy adoption. While these tools ensure compliance, modern designs increasingly combine strict mandates with market flexibility to encourage innovation. Through global examples like South Korea, Pakistan, and Barbados, we examine how well-designed regulations can deliver climate goals, economic benefits, and energy justice, especially in developing contexts where policy clarity and institutional capacity are vital.

Through global examples like South Korea, Pakistan, and Barbados, we examine how well-designed regulations can deliver climate goals, economic benefits, and energy justice, especially in developing contexts where policy clarity and institutional capacity are vital.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Market-Based Instruments
lessons.aiPrompt: Market-Based Instruments (MBIs) use prices and incentives to drive clean energy and reduce emissions. Unlike rigid regulations, they let actors choose cost-effective solutions. This lesson covers key MBIs—Feed-in Tariffs, Emissions Trading Systems, and Carbon Taxes—and shows how they promote innovation, attract investment, and support flexible, efficient energy transitions.
lessons.description: Market-Based Instruments (MBIs) use prices and incentives to drive clean energy and reduce emissions. Unlike rigid regulations, they let actors choose cost-effective solutions. This lesson covers key MBIs—Feed-in Tariffs, Emissions Trading Systems, and Carbon Taxes—and shows how they promote innovation, attract investment, and support flexible, efficient energy transitions.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Key Contractual Tools
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson explores contractual and procurement instruments—such as Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs), Independent Power Producers (IPPs), Public–Private Partnerships (PPPs), and Build–Own–Operate/Transfer (BOO/BOT) models, as key tools to mobilize private capital for sustainable energy. These instruments structure public-private relationships, reduce risks, and enable long-term investments. In developing countries, where public funds are limited, their effective use is essential to accelerate clean energy deployment and ensure a just energy transition.

chapters.title: Chapter 1: Foundational Concepts of Energy Data and Its Role in Sustainability and Equity

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Introduction to Energy Data
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson breaks down the key qualities of energy data‚ like accuracy, reliability, and timeliness‚ and why they matter for making better decisions. It also looks at how data fits into the policy-making process, from spotting problems and setting goals to tracking progress and checking results. It shows how data can help shape energy policies that work for everyone.
lessons.description: This lesson breaks down the key qualities of energy data‚ like accuracy, reliability, and timeliness‚ and why they matter for making better decisions. It also looks at how data fits into the policy-making process, from spotting problems and setting goals to tracking progress and checking results. It shows how data can help shape energy policies that work for everyone.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: The Role of Data in Advancing Sustainable Energy And Equity
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson highlights the critical role of data in advancing sustainable energy and equity, emphasizing its use to track progress, measure impacts, and address disparities. Key tools include UNDP's Energy Moonshot Tracker for monitoring global energy access initiatives and the World Bank's Multi-Tier Framework to assess energy equity beyond basic metrics. It underscores the need for transparent reporting and ethical data practices to ensure accountability, build trust, and inform policies that balance sustainability with social justice.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Approaches to Planning, Monitoring, and Evaluation in Sustainable Energy

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Strategic Energy Planning at The Policy Level
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson highlights strategic energy planning as vital for balancing energy security, economic growth, and climate goals. It emphasizes holistic frameworks that integrate resource assessment, stakeholder engagement, and long-term objectives to align policies with sustainability. Key tools like energy system modeling enable adaptable, data-driven strategies, addressing socio-economic and environmental priorities. The focus is on dynamic planning processes that evolve with technological shifts and climate challenges, ensuring equitable, resilient pathways toward net-zero futures.

The focus is on dynamic planning processes that evolve with technological shifts and climate challenges, ensuring equitable, resilient pathways toward net-zero futures.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Fundamentals of Project Planning, Monitoring, and Evaluation
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson highlights the fundamentals of project planning, monitoring, and evaluation (PM&E) as essential tools for advancing sustainable energy goals. Rooted in principles like local ownership, stakeholder inclusivity, and outcome-focused effectiveness, PM&E fosters transparency, accountability, and adaptability, ensuring energy projects deliver equitable, sustainable results through iterative learning and evidence-based decision-making.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Gender and Intersectionality in Energy Data and Planning
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson examines the integration of gender and intersectionality into energy data and planning, emphasizing the systemic disparities faced by marginalized groups. It explores challenges in sex-disaggregated data collection, gender-responsive policy design, and inclusive participatory approaches to decision-making. The lesson underscores the need for intersectional analysis to address overlapping inequalities and ensure equitable energy access and planning.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Tools and Techniques for Data-Driven Insights in Energy

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Data Science Techniques for Energy Analysis
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson explores data science techniques for energy analysis, covering exploratory data analysis (EDA) methods like anomaly detection and predictive modeling. It highlights AI-driven automation in data cleaning, remote sensing integration with GIS for renewable energy optimization, and the role of data visualization in translating complex energy data into actionable insights. The lesson emphasizes leveraging these tools to enhance decision-making and sustainability in the energy sector.
lessons.description: This lesson explores data science techniques for energy analysis, covering exploratory data analysis (EDA) methods like anomaly detection and predictive modeling. It highlights AI-driven automation in data cleaning, remote sensing integration with GIS for renewable energy optimization, and the role of data visualization in translating complex energy data into actionable insights. The lesson emphasizes leveraging these tools to enhance decision-making and sustainability in the energy sector.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Energy Modelling Tools and Applications
lessons.aiPrompt: This lesson explores energy modelling tools and their applications in analyzing and optimizing energy systems, covering descriptive, prescriptive, and predictive models for policy analysis, scenario planning, and load forecasting. It highlights renewable energy integration, grid stability, and the role of data value chains in ensuring accurate models to support sustainable energy decision-making and efficient resource management.
lessons.description: This lesson explores energy modelling tools and their applications in analyzing and optimizing energy systems, covering descriptive, prescriptive, and predictive models for policy analysis, scenario planning, and load forecasting. It highlights renewable energy integration, grid stability, and the role of data value chains in ensuring accurate models to support sustainable energy decision-making and efficient resource management.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Applied Machine Learning in Energy Data
lessons.aiPrompt: This section introduces the use of Machine Learning (ML) in energy policy, focusing on structured data, learning models, and ethical concerns. It covers key applications like energy forecasting and emissions profiling using tools such as Random Forests and clustering. Foundational concepts are linked to real-world use cases to support evidence-based policymaking. The lesson ends by addressing ethical risks and promoting best practices like Explainable AI and Privacy by Design.

Learners will gain tools to foster innovation ecosystems and lead transformative change across the global energy landscape.

modules.title: Derisking Renewable Energy Investment
modules.aiPrompt: Module 9 introduces the UNDP’s Derisking Renewable Energy Investment (DREI) framework, which identifies key risks and barriers to renewable energy investment in developing countries. It covers both utility-scale and decentralised technologies, with a focus on Sub-Saharan Africa, and explains how risk affects project finance and cost of capital before outlining the DREI framework and its main stages.

lessons.title: Module 9 Introduction

lessons.description: Introduction and learning objectives for Module 9

chapters.title: Chapter 1: Trends and Barriers for Renewable Energy in the Global South

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Renewables Are Reshaping the Global South
lessons.aiPrompt: Presents key trends, drivers, and barriers for wind and solar PV development in developing countries, linking to broader energy access and investment challenges.
lessons.description: Presents key trends, drivers, and barriers for wind and solar PV development in developing countries, linking to broader energy access and investment challenges.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Beyond the Grid: Off-Grid Innovations Lighting Up Remote Communities
lessons.aiPrompt: Explores decentralized renewable energy systems—mini-grids, rooftop solar, and Solar Home Systems—highlighting their characteristics, opportunities, limitations, and supporting initiatives.
lessons.description: Explores decentralized renewable energy systems—mini-grids, rooftop solar, and Solar Home Systems—highlighting their characteristics, opportunities, limitations, and supporting initiatives.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: From Capital to Kilowatts: How Renewable Projects Get Funded
lessons.aiPrompt: Introduces core concepts of project finance for renewable energy, covering financing structures, sources of capital, and financial appraisal tools relevant to scaling up private investment.
lessons.description: Introduces core concepts of project finance for renewable energy, covering financing structures, sources of capital, and financial appraisal tools relevant to scaling up private investment.

lessons.title: Chapter 1 Outro
lessons.aiPrompt: This chapter examines renewable energy investment in developing countries with a focus on solar photovoltaic and wind power—both utility-scale and decentralized systems.
lessons.description: Outro of the chapter and wrap-up.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Derisking Theory

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Seeing the Risk: Why Perception Shapes Investment Decisions
lessons.aiPrompt: Defines investment risks in renewable energy, explains how risk perception affects cost of capital, and outlines major risk categories for utility-scale and decentralized projects.
lessons.description: Defines investment risks in renewable energy, explains how risk perception affects cost of capital, and outlines major risk categories for utility-scale and decentralized projects.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: From Risk to Results: The DREI Theory of Change
lessons.aiPrompt: Explains the Theory of Change behind DREI, showing how public interventions can reduce investment barriers and unlock private capital for renewable energy.
lessons.description: Explains the Theory of Change behind DREI, showing how public interventions can reduce investment barriers and unlock private capital for renewable energy.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Shared Solutions: How Governments and Markets Tackle Investment Barriers
lessons.aiPrompt: Distinguishes between public and private derisking strategies, describing how each can address investment risks and improve project bankability.
lessons.description: Distinguishes between public and private derisking strategies, describing how each can address investment risks and improve project bankability.

lessons.title: Chapter 2 Outro
lessons.aiPrompt: This chapter introduces the DREI framework’s Theory of Change by examining investment risks in renewable energy, their impact on capital costs, and strategies to address them
lessons.description: Outro of the chapter and wrap-up.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: The DREI Framework

lessons.title: Lesson 1: DREI in Practice: Origins, Structure, and Impact
lessons.aiPrompt: Provides an overview of the DREI framework’s origins, structure, and practical applications for improving renewable energy investment conditions through targeted public interventions.
lessons.description: Provides an overview of the DREI framework’s origins, structure, and practical applications for improving renewable energy investment conditions through targeted public interventions.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Understanding the Risk Environment: What Shapes Renewable Investment?
lessons.aiPrompt: Explains how to assess the risk environment for renewable energy investment, including stakeholder engagement, risk categorization, and analysis of financing costs.
lessons.description: Explains how to assess the risk environment for renewable energy investment, including stakeholder engagement, risk categorization, and analysis of financing costs.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: The Public Sector’s Toolkit: Making Renewable Investment Possible
lessons.aiPrompt: Describes the identification and evaluation of public derisking instruments, showing how policy and financial tools can reduce investment risks and improve financing terms.
lessons.description: Describes the identification and evaluation of public derisking instruments, showing how policy and financial tools can reduce investment risks and improve financing terms.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: LCOE Modelling: Comparing Costs, Guiding Decisions
lessons.aiPrompt: Covers levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) modeling within the DREI framework, demonstrating how risk mitigation and public instruments can lower renewable energy costs and enhance competitiveness.
lessons.description: Covers levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) modeling within the DREI framework, demonstrating how risk mitigation and public instruments can lower renewable energy costs and enhance competitiveness.

lessons.title: Chapter 3 Outro
lessons.aiPrompt: This learning module introduces the DREI framework, guiding learners through its origins, key analytical components, and practical applications to assess risks, design public interventions, and support renewable energy investment planning.
lessons.description: Outro of the chapter and wrap-up.
